name: What makes a door installation truly weatherproof?

mainEntity.name: Which sealing and flashing steps separate a basic install from a weatherproof one?
mainEntity.text: Which sealing and flashing steps separate a basic install from a weatherproof one?

acceptedAnswer.text: A weatherproof door installation is more than putting a new slab into an opening. It starts with a square, stable frame and continues with proper flashing, sill protection, shimming, and sealing around the perimeter. The installer should also use exterior-grade caulk, compressible insulation where appropriate, and weatherstripping that fits tightly without preventing the door from closing smoothly. The threshold and bottom seal are especially important in rainy, windy areas like Pacifica and Half Moon Bay, where water can be driven into weak points. Good weatherproofing also depends on the right fasteners and on making sure the frame is anchored securely so it does not shift over time. If you are comparing bids, ask the contractor to explain exactly how they will keep water out, not just whether the door is “sealed.
acceptedAnswer.description: A weatherproof installation uses proper flashing, sill sealing, shims, exterior caulking, insulation around the frame, and correctly fitted weatherstripping. The door must also be square and securely anchored. Without those steps, even an expensive door can leak air and water.
